coredata.dc:title: Theoretical study on electronic properties of curved graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Pattarapongdilok, Naruwan
Parasuk, Vudhichai
coredata.dc:description: Electronic properties of curved graphene quantum dots (CGQDs) were studied using Density Functional Theory (DFT) method. Two shapes of the zigzag-edged CGQDs, i.e., rhomboidal (RGQDs) and hexagonal (HGQDs) graphene quantum dots, and each with five sizes were studied. For each GQD, its structure was curved along symmetry-unique folding axes at 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 140, and 160. There are four unique folding axes (FA1, FA2, FA3, and FA4) for RGQDS and only two axes for HGQDs (FA1 and FA2). The results showed that HOMO and LUMO energies of CGQDs depend on size, shape, folding axis, and folding degree. The band gap (HOMO-LUMO gap) at different folding degrees were monitored for all structures. For the folding at FA1, FA2, and FA4 of RGQDs and FA1 and FA2 of HGQDs, the narrowing of the band gap was observed, while only for the folding at FA3 of RGQDs the band gap widening was found. Orbital interactions can explain the narrowing/widening of the band gap of CGQDs. The energy used for curving GQDs (the deformation energy) depends on the size and folding axis but not their shape. The “armchair-like” folding has higher deformation energy than the “zigzag-like” one. The deformation energy is related to the folding strain and the number of the same-symmetry axis. The number of the same-symmetry axis is also related to the size of GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Curved graphene
Ripple graphene
Graphene quantum dots
HOMO-LUMO gap

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of ripples on electronic properties of CGQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Center of Excellence in Computational Chemistry of Chulalongkorn University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: N. Pattarapongdilok would like to thank Center of Innovative Nanotechnology (CIN), Chulalongkorn University and Bansomdejchaopraya Rajabhat University for financial support. Also, the support of computer resources and other facilities by the computer science program at Bansomdejchaopraya Rajabhat University and the Center of Excellence in Computational Chemistry of Chulalongkorn University must be as well acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Distinct folding axes of (a) RGQDs and (b) HGQDs.
Change in HOMO-LUMO gap of RGQDs with folding axes and folding angles; (a) a2 × 2 structures, (b) a3 × 3 structures, (c) a4 × 4 structures, (d) a5 × 5 structures, (e) a6 × 6 structures.
Relation between folding angle and average sp2 angle: (a) a4 × 4 rhomboidal-shape CGQDs; (b) b4 × 4 hexagonal-shape CGQDs.
Comparison of HOMO and LUMO energies of RGQDs at 2 and 16 degrees of (a) a3 × 3FA2, (b) a3 × 3FA3.
Illustrations of HOMO and LUMO orbitals of a3 × 3 RGQD for (a) FA2 and (b) FA3 folding.
Side view structures of a6 × 6 RGQD at the folding angle of 14°when folded along (a) FA2 and (b) FA3.
Changes in HOMO-LUMO gap of HGQDs with folding axes and folding angles; (a) b2x2 structure, (b) b3 × 3 structure, (c) b4 × 4 structure, (d) b5 × 5 structure, (e) b6 × 6 structure.
Comparison of HOMO and LUMO energies of HGQDs at 12 and 14 degrees of (a) b3 × 3FA1 and (b) b3 × 3FA2.
Comparison of HOMO and LUMO orbitals of b3 × 3 HGQD; (a) FA1 and (b) FA2.
Comparison of (a) non-ripple and (b) ripple CGQD structures at the degree θ.
Deformation energies for different folding axes of RGQDs at various folding angles; (a) a2 × 2 structures, (b) a3 × 3 structures, (c) a4 × 4 structures, (d) a5 × 5 structures, (e) a6 × 6 structures.
Deformation energies for different folding axes of HGQDs at various folding angles; (a) b2 × 2 structures, (b) b3 × 3 structures, (c) b4 × 4 structures, (d) b5 × 5 structures, (e) b6 × 6 structures.
HOMO and LUMO energies and HOMO-LUMO gaps for ripple and non-ripple curved RGQDs and HGQDs folded at 2 and 16°along armchair-like folding axes.

head.ce:title: Theoretical study on electronic properties of curved graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanoscience and Technology Program, Graduate School, Chulalongkorn University, Bangkok 10330, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Excellence in Computational Chemistry, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Chulalongkorn University, Bangkok 10330, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Excellence in Computational Chemistry

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Chulalongkorn University, Bangkok 10330, Thailand.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electronic properties of curved graphene quantum dots (CGQDs) were studied using Density Functional Theory (DFT) method. Two shapes of the zigzag-edged CGQDs, i.e., rhomboidal (RGQDs) and hexagonal (HGQDs) graphene quantum dots, and each with five sizes were studied. For each GQD, its structure was curved along symmetry-unique folding axes at 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 140, and 160. There are four unique folding axes (FA1, FA2, FA3, and FA4) for RGQDS and only two axes for HGQDs (FA1 and FA2). The results showed that HOMO and LUMO energies of CGQDs depend on size, shape, folding axis, and folding degree. The band gap (HOMO-LUMO gap) at different folding degrees were monitored for all structures. For the folding at FA1, FA2, and FA4 of RGQDs and FA1 and FA2 of HGQDs, the narrowing of the band gap was observed, while only for the folding at FA3 of RGQDs the band gap widening was found. Orbital interactions can explain the narrowing/widening of the band gap of CGQDs. The energy used for curving GQDs (the deformation energy) depends on the size and folding axis but not their shape. The “armchair-like” folding has higher deformation energy than the “zigzag-like” one. The deformation energy is related to the folding strain and the number of the same-symmetry axis. The number of the same-symmetry axis is also related to the size of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene is a two-dimensional monolayer of sp2-hybridized carbons. Its hexagonal network, honeycomb lattice, is a primary building block for potential materials such as three-dimensional graphite, one-dimensional nanotubes, and zero-dimensional fullerenes [1–5]. Nowadays, graphene is applied to electronics and hi-technology devices. Because it has remarkable properties such as one-atom thinness, excellent transmittance and conductivity, extremely high stiffness, strong elasticity, and high thermal stability [6,7]. Bao et al. [8] using SEM and AFM techniques to study graphene membrane found that all graphene show periodic ripple. This is attributed to the strong in-plane (covalent bond) and weak out-of-plane (van der Waals) interactions of graphene [9]. Curved graphene has also been made. Several approaches such as mechanical exfoliation [10], AFM tip [11], chemical oxidation following by thermal treatment [12], printing or coating on a curved surface [8,13,14] have been reported to generate curved graphene. It has been shown that the deformation or curvature or ripple can change the optical and electronic properties of graphene [9–11,15–21]. From this behavior, we can make graphene into strain sensors [15,21] and novel electronic devices [13,22]. Graphene nanoribbons (GNRs) is the strip of graphene with the length of >10 nm and the width of <50 nm [23]. Zhang et al. [24] have reported that the curvature affects the electronic property of GNRs and found the band gap energy of GNRs to decrease at the increment of the curvature. Chang et al. [25] have studied the strain energy of curved GNRs and found it to become smaller for larger GNRs. Thus, the curvature influences the electronic and optical properties of graphene and GNRs.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can be a single- or few-layer of graphene having dimensions in the range of a few nm to
100 nm in diameter, normally below 20 nm [26,27]. It can be prepared by top-down and bottom-up methods. In the top-down approach, GQD is generated from the decomposition and exfoliation of bulk graphene-based materials such as graphite. The process involves the use of concentrated acids, strong oxidizing agents, and high temperatures [28–33]. On the other hand, the bottom-up approach is achieved by the synthesis of aromatic molecules such as fullerenes, which often involves complicated reaction steps and specific organic reagents. However, this method can control the sizes and properties of the final products [28–33]. Recently, GQDs are being interested in bio-sensing, display, and energy applications. Because they have combined properties of graphene and quantum dots, such as high specific surface area, high mobility, good biocompatibility, strong luminescence, good hole transport ability, high solubility in various solvents, low toxicity, etc. [26,34–39]. Ritter et al. have found the decrease of the energy band gap when the size of GQD increases for both zigzag-edge and armchair-edge GQDs [40]. Apart from experimental evidence, there have been several theoretical studies on edge and size effects of GQDs [41–43]. Results from these theoretical works also support the finding of Ritter et al. Thus, the band gap of GQDs is tunable by modifying its size, edge, surface, and geometry [26,37,40,44] which leads to desired conductivity. Not only for flat GQDs, but electronic properties of curved GQDs (CGQDs) are also of interest. So far, there have been very limited numbers of studies on electronic properties of CGQDs. It has been reported that the electronic properties of GNRs can be affected by their sizes and edge similarly to GQDs [40]. Thus, we expect the curvature effect on electronic properties of GQDs to be the same as those of GNRs.

Unlike graphene, GQD is much smaller in size. Its properties depend on the size and locality, which make this material very interesting for applications. Since the electronic property such as the band gap energy is related to the optical property, it is then allowed GQD to be used as optical devices.

In this work, we focus on the effect of curvature deriving from folding axes and folding degrees as well as the shape and size of flat GQDs on their electronic properties. This understanding could then be applied to the design of GQDs-based electronic devices as well as strain sensors and optical devices.
Two structural analogs of GQDs, i.e., rhomboidal- (RGQDs) and hexagonal- (HGQDs) shapes as shown in Fig. 1 were studied.

Initial GQD structures were created from a single layer of graphite taken from the library of the Material Studio 5.5 program [45]. Models of RGQDs denoted by anxn were generated from n x n hexagonal units, where n = 2–6. All dangling C atoms were terminated by H atom giving C16H10 (a2 × 2), C30H14 (a3 × 3), C48H18 (a4 × 4), C70H22 (a5 × 5), and C96H26 (a6 × 6), Fig. 1a. Three n x n rhombic structures can represent the hexagonal-shape. Models of HGQDs are denoted by bnxn for n = 2–6 and their terminal carbons were saturated by H atoms. Thus, they consist of C24H12 (b2 × 2), C54H18 (b3 × 3), C96H24 (b4 × 4), C150H30 (b5 × 5), and C216H36 (b6 × 6), Fig. 1b. Geometries of GQDs were fully optimized using DFT (Density Functional Theory) [46] with Perdew, Burke, and Ernzerhof (PBE) [47] functional and double numerical plus polarization (DNP) basis set [48,49] of DMol3 program in Material Studio 5.5 suite. To create CGQDs, geometrically optimized GQDs were curved or folded along designated axes at the incremental of 2 to 16°.

Folding axes were defined from the unique atom-to-atom direction in the hexagonal unit (Fig. 2 ). There are four distinct folding axes, FA1, FA2, FA3, and FA4, for RGQDs and only two distinct folding axes, FA1 and FA2 for HGQDs (Fig. 2). However, it should be noticed that for a particular distinct folding axis there is more than one axis of the same symmetry or type. The number of “same-symmetry” axis depends on the folding axis and the size of GQDs. To curve the GQD to a certain degree along with a particular folding axis, all same-symmetry axes of that type will be folded simultaneously at the designated degree. It happens that for RGQDs the folding by FA1 and FA4 axes is through carbon atoms at the zigzag position (“zigzag” carbons), while the folding by FA2 and FA3 axes is through “armchair” carbons. We denoted the former by the “zigzag-like folding” and it has equal spacing between the same-symmetry axes. The latter is denoted by the “armchair-like folding” where it has the alternated short and long spacing between the same-symmetry axes. For HGQDs, the folding by FA1 belongs to the zigzag-like folding and by FA2 fits the armchair-like folding. This observation is also shared by Casabianca [50].
For CGQDs, only positions of hydrogen atoms were optimized at the same level of theory as those of GQDs. HOMO and LUMO energies of GQDs and CGQDs were also obtained at the same level of theory.

The reliance of HOMO-LUMO gaps of RGQDs on their sizes, folding axes, and folding angles was observed and displayed in Fig. 3 . However, this variance of HOMO-LUMO gaps with folding angles is different from the axis to axis. A more profound change on HOMO-LUMO gaps was found when the structure is folded along the FA3/FA2 axis for all sizes of RGQDs. Thus, we can categorize the folding axis into two groups based on this behavior, i.e., FA2/FA3 and FA1/FA4. This grouping is, somehow, corresponded to the armchair-like and the zigzag-like folding or the way that GQDs are folded.

The dependence of HOMO-LUMO gaps on folding angles for a particular folding axis happens to be the same as the relation between folding angles and average CCC or Csp2 angles of the hexagonal ring along that axis, Fig. 4 . (The CCC angle is referred to the bond angle between C atom in the hexagonal ring and the Csp2 angle is the angle of the sp2 hybrid orbital of C.)

In Fig. 4a, the average Csp2 angle for FA1, FA2, FA3, and FA4 of a4 × 4 were plotted against the folding degree. We found that plots of average Csp2 angles for FA2 and FA3 are wholly aligned with each other and they deviate from the theoretical value of 120° when folded. A similar observation was found for those of FA1 and FA4, but their plots are well distinct from those of FA2/FA3. It is interesting to note that the average Csp2 angle depends only on the folding axis but not with the size of RGQDs (see Table S1 in the Supplementary Information). Also, we noticed the different behavior of the HOMO-LUMO gap variation for FA3 to other folding axes. For FA3, the band gap increases at the advancement of the folding degree while it is reduced for other axes. This band gap widening upon folding along FA3 is also different from that observed by Zhang et al., who found that the HOMO-LUMO gap of graphene nanoribbons becomes narrower with the greater curvature [24]. This makes an interesting electronic property for curved RGQDs. The behavior of the HOMO-LUMO gap widening/narrowing upon folding is better realized in Fig. 5 . In Fig. 5, HOMO/LUMO energies of a3 × 3 when the structure is folded by 2 and 16°along FA2 and FA3 are displayed.
Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.comptc.2018.08.002.

From Fig. 5, it can be seen that the folding along FA2 and FA3 destabilizes both HOMO and LUMO of a3 × 3 RGQD. For FA2, we observed a significant increase in HOMO energy, while its LUMO is slightly decreased. Hence, this results in the narrowing of the HOMO-LUMO gap. However, for FA3 both HOMO and LUMO energies are significantly raised, but the increase of HOMO is less than that of LUMO. Therefore, the band gap widening is observed. To understand how the folding affecting HOMO/LUMO energies, plots of HOMO and LUMO orbitals at two different folding angles, 2° and 16°, for FA2 and FA3 were carefully analyzed. Fig. 6 illustrates three views, i.e., top, side, and bottom, of HOMO and LUMO orbitals for FA2 and FA3 at the folding angle of 2° and 16°. The dotted line represents the folding axis, while light grey and dark grey colors denote the orbital phase.

From Fig. 6, it could be observed that upon folding two π-orbitals with different phases were brought towards each other. Thus, the folding causes the repulsion of electron clouds and raises (destabilizes) the orbital energy. For LUMO of FA2 folding, the π orbitals are not exactly head on to each other but just in the near vicinity. While for LUMO of FA3 folding these orbitals are in the head-on position. Therefore, there is a stronger orbital interaction (electron cloud repulsion) for FA3 folding and its LUMO energy is more significantly increase. The same explanation can be used for the HOMO of FA2 folding. Since by FA2 folding the π-orbitals in the HOMO are aligned much closer than in the LUMO, the HOMO is thus more strongly affected by the orbital interactions. The significant increase of HOMO upon folding is then seen. Instead of bringing π-orbitals with opposite phase towards each other like in other cases, the FA3 folding breaks π bonds of HOMO when the structure is folded, which is followed by the rise of HOMO energy. This is the different mode of orbital interactions from the case of LUMO of FA3 folding. Thus, the increase of orbital energy is less than the LUMO. Since the folding axis assigns how the orbital interaction occurs, the behavior of the band gap on the folding angle (either widening or narrowing) is derived from the folding axis. Thus, the discrepancy between our study (FA3 of RGQDs) and that of Zhang et al. [24] likely comes from the different choice of the folding axis.

The discontinuity in the HOMO-LUMO gap as shown in Fig. 3(e) was observed when a6x6 RGQD was folded by 14° and beyond along FA3. This could also be explained by the orbital interaction. At 14°, the folding along FA3 bring two ends of RGQD very close to each other as illustrated in Fig. 7 (b). While for the other folding axis such as FA2, two ends of RGQD remain sufficiently apart, Fig. 7(a). Thus, only the folding along FA3 shows discontinuity in the HOMO-LUMO gap. Our hypothesis is supported by the fact that the folding at 16° could not be achieved for this structure since two ends of RGQD will be overlaid and no convergence solution could be found. The same argument can also be used to describe Fig. 3(d).

For HGQDs, two folding axes, FA1 and FA2, are their classes as demonstrated in Fig. 4(b). The average sp2 angle of FA1 is higher than that of FA2. The explanation of zigzag- and armchair-like folding as given for FA1/FA4 and FA2/FA3 of RGQDs can be as well applied. Like RGQDs, the average sp2 angle for HGQDs do not vary with their sizes (see Table S2 in the Supplementary Information). From Fig. 8 , the HOMO-LUMO gap of HGQDs is reduced when the degree of folding was raised for both FA1 and FA2 at all quantum dot sizes. However, the HOMO-LUMO gap of the folding along FA2 declines faster.

For HGQDs, two folding axes, FA1 and FA2, are their classes as demonstrated in Fig. 4(b). The average sp2 angle of FA1 is higher than that of FA2. The explanation of zigzag- and armchair-like folding as given for FA1/FA4 and FA2/FA3 of RGQDs can be as well applied. Like RGQDs, the average sp2 angle for HGQDs do not vary with their sizes (see Table S2 in the Supplementary Information). From Fig. 8 , the HOMO-LUMO gap of HGQDs is reduced when the degree of folding was raised for both FA1 and FA2 at all quantum dot sizes. However, the HOMO-LUMO gap of the folding along FA2 declines faster.

Similar to RGQDs, the lowering of the HOMO-LUMO gap comes from the greater destabilization of HOMO than LUMO, see Fig. 9 . This increase in HOMO energy upon folding is related to the orbital interactions as shown in Fig. 10 . The similar explanation as given for FA2 of RGQDs can also be used for this case.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of ripples on electronic properties of CGQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Due to its nature, most of generated graphene have ripple structures. The ripple itself causes the change in electronic properties from flat graphene. Hence, it is quite interesting to investigate the effect of ripples on electronic properties of CGQDs. Here, to generate the ripple CGQD we folded the GQD at the designated same-symmetry axis by the angle of −θ, while the other same-symmetry axes were folded by θ, see Fig. 11 .

HOMO and LUMO energies, as well as HOMO-LUMO gaps for ripple and non-ripple curved RGQDs and HGQDs, folded at 2 and 16°along their armchair-like folding axes were presented in Table 1 . It appears that the ripple does not alter the effect of curvature on GQDs. The widening/narrowing of the band gap upon folding for a particular folding axis remains. For the small folding degree, the ripple causes a negligible change in both HOMO and LUMO energies. As a result, no significant change in the HOMO-LUMO gap was observed. However, for the large folding angle the escalation of HOMO/LUMO energy for a3 × 3 FA2 and b3 × 3 FA2 and the lowering of HOMO/LUMO energy for a3 × 3 FA3 were noticed. Since the changes in HOMO and LUMO energies are in the same direction for all folding axes, thus the widening/narrowing of the band gap upon folding along a particular folding axis is preserved. We also observed a more significant change for HOMO than LUMO. The slightly smaller HOMO-LUMO gap was seen for all axes at the larger folding angle. Again, the change in the HOMO/LUMO gap of ripple CGQDs can be explained by the orbital interactions, see Figs. S2–S4 in the supplementary.

HOMO and LUMO energies, as well as HOMO-LUMO gaps for ripple and non-ripple curved RGQDs and HGQDs, folded at 2 and 16°along their armchair-like folding axes were presented in Table 1 . It appears that the ripple does not alter the effect of curvature on GQDs. The widening/narrowing of the band gap upon folding for a particular folding axis remains. For the small folding degree, the ripple causes a negligible change in both HOMO and LUMO energies. As a result, no significant change in the HOMO-LUMO gap was observed. However, for the large folding angle the escalation of HOMO/LUMO energy for a3 × 3 FA2 and b3 × 3 FA2 and the lowering of HOMO/LUMO energy for a3 × 3 FA3 were noticed. Since the changes in HOMO and LUMO energies are in the same direction for all folding axes, thus the widening/narrowing of the band gap upon folding along a particular folding axis is preserved. We also observed a more significant change for HOMO than LUMO. The slightly smaller HOMO-LUMO gap was seen for all axes at the larger folding angle. Again, the change in the HOMO/LUMO gap of ripple CGQDs can be explained by the orbital interactions, see Figs. S1–S4 in the supplementary.

The deformation energy is defined by the difference between the total energy of CGQD and flat GQD. The value represents the ease of curving GQDs along a particular folding axis. The plot between deformation energies and folding angles for various folding axes of RGQDs and HGQDs were displayed in Figs. 12 and 13
, respectively.

As might be expected, the rise of the deformation energy with the degree of folding along all folding axes and for all sizes of GQDs was observed. Interestingly, the relation between deformation energies and folding angles is harmonic (R2 ∼ 0.99 when fits with the quadratic equation). This increment of the deformation energy with folding angles is accorded with the report of Chang et al. [25] on curved graphene nanoribbons. The deformation energy also depends on the folding axis of GQDs. It requires more energy to bend along the armchair-like folding axis (FA2, FA3 for RGQDs and FA2 for HGQDs) than that along the zigzag-like folding axis (FA1, FA4 for RGQDs and FA1 for HGQDs). This might be because the armchair-like folding generates higher “folding” strain. Since folded CC bonds for the armchair-like folding make 30° with the folding axis, while they make 60° for the zigzag-like folding, see Fig. 2. Furthermore, we found that for the same type of folding axis the deformation energy is depended on the size of GQDs only, not on their shape. Because when we make the plot between the deformation energy at 16° of RGQDs (FA3) and HGQDs (FA2) with numbers of carbon atoms, the linear equation with R2 of 0.997 was obtained, see Supplementary Information Fig. S1. Numbers of the same-symmetry axis can explain this increase of the deformation energy with the size of GQDs since the larger GQD has the higher numbers of the same-symmetry axis.

As might be expected, the rise of the deformation energy with the degree of folding along all folding axes and for all sizes of GQDs was observed. Interestingly, the relation between deformation energies and folding angles is harmonic (R2 ∼ 0.99 when fits with the quadratic equation). This increment of the deformation energy with folding angles is accorded with the report of Chang et al. [25] on curved graphene nanoribbons. The deformation energy also depends on the folding axis of GQDs. It requires more energy to bend along the armchair-like folding axis (FA2, FA3 for RGQDs and FA2 for HGQDs) than that along the zigzag-like folding axis (FA1, FA4 for RGQDs and FA1 for HGQDs). This might be because the armchair-like folding generates higher “folding” strain. Since folded CC bonds for the armchair-like folding make 30° with the folding axis, while they make 60° for the zigzag-like folding, see Fig. 2. Furthermore, we found that for the same type of folding axis the deformation energy is depended on the size of GQDs only, not on their shape. Because when we make the plot between the deformation energy at 16° of RGQDs (FA3) and HGQDs (FA2) with numbers of carbon atoms, the linear equation with R2 of 0.997 was obtained, see Supplementary Information Fig. S1 . Numbers of the same-symmetry axis can explain this increase of the deformation energy with the size of GQDs since the larger GQD has the higher numbers of the same-symmetry axis.

HOMO-LUMO or band gaps of CGQDs depend on shapes, sizes, folding axes, and degrees of folding. We observed that the band gap of GQD is decreased at the increment of folding angles for all folding axes. This is to be excepted by the folding along FA3 of RGQDs where the band gaps increase. The orbital interactions could describe the change of band gap and HOMO/LUMO energies upon folding. The orbital interaction causes the destabilization of orbitals. Thus, HOMO/LUMO energies are raised upon folding. For FA3 of RGQDs, the folding destabilizes LUMO more than HOMO and the widening of the band gap is observed. For other cases, HOMO is destabilized than LUMO and the band gap narrowing is found. The magnitude of the band gap change depends on types of the folding axis. A more significant change was observed for the armchair-like folding (FA2/FA3 of RGQDs and FA2 of HGQDs). The ripple does not alter the widening/narrowing behavior of the band gap for CGQD. The effect of ripples on HOMO/LUMO energies is stronger at the larger folding angle causing a smaller HOMO-LUMO gap for the ripple CGQD than the non-ripple one. The folding along FA2 and FA3 of RGQDs and FA2 of HGQDs requires the higher amount of energy than that along zigzag-like folding, FA4 and FA1 of RGQDs and FA1 of HGQDs. This behavior is explained by the folding strain. We also observed that for a particular type of folding axis the deformation energy is depended on the size but not the shape of GQDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: N. Pattarapongdilok would like to thank Center of Innovative Nanotechnology (CIN), Chulalongkorn University and Bansomdejchaopraya Rajabhat University for financial support. Also, the support of computer resources and other facilities by the computer science program at Bansomdejchaopraya Rajabhat University and the Center of Excellence in Computational Chemistry of Chulalongkorn University must be as well acknowledged.
